title: Find the complement
summary: Turn a search over pairs into one forced-complement question per value, then watch the processed prefix become searchable memory.
learning_objective: Recognize that choosing the current value fixes its only useful partner, so a hash lookup can replace an inner scan.
invariant: Before every lookup, seen contains exactly the values at earlier indices—never the current index.

phases.title: Frame the search
phases.summary: Translate a pair search into a complement lookup and establish the map invariant.

phases.title: Build memory
phases.summary: Scan left to right, checking first and storing only after a miss.

phases.title: Connect the pair
phases.summary: Use the current complement to retrieve its earlier index in constant expected time.

phases.title: Return indices
phases.summary: Stop as soon as the unique valid pair is identified.

steps.title: Start from the contract
steps.description: We need two different indices whose values total 6. The result must contain positions, not the values themselves.
steps.insight: The example begins with 3, whose complement is also 3. That makes it ideal for exposing the same-index trap.

steps.title: Create searchable memory
steps.description: The map stores each processed value together with the index where it appeared.
steps.insight: At this moment the invariant is established: no index has been processed, so seen must be empty.

steps.title: Read the first value
steps.description: The scan begins at index 0, so the current value becomes 3 while seen remains empty.
steps.insight: Only the current pointer moves. No information is stored until the lookup has failed.

steps.title: Force the only useful partner
steps.description: Once value 3 is fixed, its partner is no longer unknown: 6 - 3 = 3.
steps.insight: This algebraic step removes an entire search dimension. We ask one membership question instead of trying every partner.

steps.title: Check before inserting
steps.description: Complement 3 is absent because the map still contains only earlier indices—and there are none.
steps.insight: If we inserted first, this lookup would find index 0 and incorrectly reuse the same element twice. Operation order is part of correctness.

steps.title: Remember index 0
steps.description: After the miss, store 3 → 0 so later values can find this position in expected constant time.
steps.insight: The processed prefix is now [3], and the map is an exact searchable representation of that prefix.

steps.title: Advance to index 1
steps.description: The current value changes to 2. The earlier 3 remains available in seen.
steps.insight: At the start of every iteration, the map contains earlier values only. The invariant has survived the transition.

steps.title: Compute the next need
steps.description: For current value 2, the required partner is 6 - 2 = 4.
steps.insight: The algorithm never asks whether any value might work; it computes the one value that must work.

steps.title: Search only the processed prefix
steps.description: Seen contains 3, not 4, so the required partner has not appeared yet.
steps.insight: A miss does not mean the value is useless. Storing it lets a future element discover the pair from the other direction.

steps.title: Expand searchable memory
steps.description: Store 2 → 1. Seen now represents exactly the processed prefix [3, 2].
steps.insight: Each array value is inserted at most once, which is why the total work can stay linear.

steps.title: Reach the value that completes the pair
steps.description: The scan reaches value 4 at index 2 while both earlier values are indexed in seen.
steps.insight: Predict the next complement before continuing: what must be added to 4 to reach 6?

steps.title: Derive the matching key
steps.description: For value 4, the required partner is 6 - 4 = 2.
steps.insight: The computed number is simultaneously a mathematical complement and a hash-map key.

steps.title: Retrieve the earlier index
steps.description: Key 2 is present and maps to index 1. Values 2 and 4 therefore reach the target 6.
steps.insight: The lookup proves both conditions at once: the values sum correctly, and the stored index is distinct because it is earlier.

steps.title: Return and stop
steps.description: The stored index 1 and current index 2 form the unique answer, so no later values need to be inspected.
steps.insight: One pass, one lookup per value, and immediate termination give O(n) expected time with O(n) auxiliary space.
